story:
I’m on another all-nighter, mopping a hallway that never gets dirty. The place is glossy-white, empty, and too quiet—no music, no hum, just the slap of the mop and my footsteps. I work in a sealed wing of a facility that pretends it isn’t there. Every shift starts with a body scan and a reminder of the NDA I signed months ago, yet nobody will tell me what TALE actually does. No phone, no tech, no labels on the doors. You can live here in the east wing if you follow the rules and wear a tracker when you leave. I took the deal—good money, a steady bed, and no one to miss me. My parents are gone, my friends drifted away after I burned them, and the partner I thought I’d keep chose someone else. When TALE found me at a corner shop and said I was exactly what they needed, I didn’t ask questions. They saved me, I thought.

Still, the place feels watched. I once slid down the hall like it was an ice rink—because why not—and got a pay cut announced over the intercom. No cameras, right? Sure. I started wondering if they were in my apartment too.

That’s when I heard something I never hear in this wing: footsteps—several—hurrying. The corridor bends, so I heard them before I saw them. Around the corner came two scientists in hazmat suits, each gripping a metal tool I didn’t recognize, and a businessman in a tailored, old-school suit that fit like skin. He was sweating, older, wound tight.

“You’d better get this specimen under control; we cannot afford to lose it,” he snapped, and they blew past me into an unlabeled set of double doors. Then silence came back like a wave.

Specimen. My brain started racing—formula? Prototype? Something alive? I slowed my mop without noticing. Then a sound—like an organic explosion, wet and heavy—and after that a scream, a crackle of electricity, and gunshots. My fear woke up.

The double doors hissed, and alarms started beeping. Footsteps pounded. The man in the suit burst out, one bloody hand clamped to his neck. Up close, I saw a gaping wound pumping through his fingers, which were torn and half-severed. He tried to sprint past me just as my bucket tipped—cleaner washing across the tiles. He hit the slick, slipped hard, and ripped his hand away to catch himself. The chemicals bit his mangled fingers and he choked on a scream.

“You idiot!” he yelled, then kicked my leg to push himself past. I fell sideways, cracked my head on the tile, and the corridor tilted. He staggered off, slower now, bleeding too fast to live.

The clicking started—wet, rhythmic, building. From beyond the open doors came beeps, running, and something else. Then it stepped into view: roughly human-sized but hunched, bigger than me if it stood straight. Its surface was a translucent, silvery shell—reflective, irregular—an exoskeleton with six limbs. It moved with an insect’s economy, each click faster than the last.

“Hey! Stop!” I said, backing away on shaky legs. It accelerated. I ran. Panic turned my body hollow as the clicks closed in—closer, closer—until the hallway narrowed into sound and fear and the sense of something right behind me.
